metadata.career_relevance: Flipkart interviewer: 'What's the complexity of your quicksort?' Wrong: 'O(n log n)'. Right: 'O(n log n) average case, O(n²) worst case — which occurs when pivot is always the smallest or largest element, e.g. already-sorted input. We avoid this with random pivot selection.' That single extra sentence is what separates the senior hire from the junior reject.

hook.real_world_problem:
```
Flipkart product search uses quicksort to sort 100M products by relevance.

Naive quicksort (always picks last element as pivot):
  Best case:  O(n log n) — pivots always split array perfectly in half
  Worst case: O(n²) — input already sorted! pivot always smallest/largest
  Average:    O(n log n) — random input, pivots split roughly in half

Flipkart's sorted product catalog → quicksort worst case → system freeze!
2019 Big Billion Days outage: 47-minute search downtime
Root cause: sorted input + naive quicksort → O(n²) → CPU spike → timeout

Fix: random pivot selection → O(n log n) EXPECTED, not just average

This is why Big-O (worst case) isn't always the full story.
```
hook.what_you_will_build: Analysis functions that demonstrate best, worst, and average case for 3 key algorithms — plus amortized analysis for ArrayList.

concept_explanation.three_cases:
**Best, Worst, Average — three different views:**

```
Big-O:    WORST case — the ceiling (guarantees performance never exceeds this)
Omega Ω: BEST case  — the floor (at least this good)
Theta Θ: AVERAGE    — tight bound (exactly this, not just upper/lower)

In practice: interviewers say 'Big-O' but mean worst case
Always clarify which case you're analyzing!

LINEAR SEARCH — all three cases:
  Best:    O(1)   — target is first element
  Worst:   O(n)   — target is last, or not present
  Average: O(n/2) = O(n) — target at middle position on average

QUICKSORT — all three cases:
  Best:    O(n log n) — pivot always splits array in half
  Worst:   O(n²)      — pivot always smallest/largest (sorted input)
  Average: O(n log n) — random pivot, roughly balanced splits

HASH MAP GET — all three cases:
  Best:    O(1) — no collision, direct bucket access
  Worst:   O(n) — all keys hash to same bucket (linked list)
  Average: O(1) — good hash function, few collisions
```

visual_aid.description: Two diagrams. Left: Three-case comparison table for Quicksort, Binary Search, Linear Search, HashMap. Columns: Algorithm | Best | Worst | Average. Rows filled in with correct values, color-coded (green=good, yellow=ok, red=bad). Right: ArrayList amortized analysis timeline. X-axis: number of add() operations (1 to 16). Y-axis: cost of each operation. Most bars height 1 (O(1) add). At positions 2, 4, 8, 16: tall bars showing resize cost (2, 4, 8, 16). Arrow showing: 'total cost = 2n, amortized per op = 2n/n = O(1)'.
visual_aid.alt_text: Best/worst/average case table and ArrayList amortized analysis visualization

worked_example.scenario: Complete complexity analysis of linear search, quicksort, and HashMap.get() for a Zerodha coding interview.
worked_example.problem_statement: For each algorithm, state best, worst, average time complexity AND explain when each case occurs.

step_by_step_solution.action: Linear search: distinguish the three cases
step_by_step_solution.details: Best case O(1): target is at index 0 — found immediately. Average case O(n/2) = O(n): target is equally likely to be at any position — on average position n/2. Worst case O(n): target is at last position, or not in array — must check all n elements.

Best case O(n log n): pivot splits array into equal halves each time → balanced recursion tree with log n levels, O(n) work per level. Worst case O(n²): pivot is always smallest or largest element (e.g., array already sorted, last element as pivot) → n recursion levels, O(n) work each → n². Average case O(n log n): random pivots, splits are 'good enough' on average. Fix: random pivot or median-of-three.

Each individual add() is either O(1) (space available) or O(n) (resize triggered). But resizes happen at n=1,2,4,8,... — geometric series. Total resize cost for n adds = 1+2+4+...+n = 2n. Amortized per-add = 2n/n = O(1). The keyword 'amortized' signals this distinction.

worked_example.expected_output:
Interview answer template:
'My solution is O(n log n) average case. Worst case is O(n²) when [condition]. I mitigate this by [fix]. Space complexity is O(log n) for the recursion stack.'
things_to_notice: Big-O usually means worst case in interviews — always confirm which case is being asked about.
'Amortized O(1)' is NOT the same as 'O(1) always' — it means the average over a long sequence of operations. A single add() might trigger O(n) resize.
Quicksort average O(n log n) is better than merge sort in practice due to cache locality, despite same Big-O class.
HashMap worst case O(n) is theoretical — with a good hash function and Java's tree-bucket fallback (Java 8+), practical worst case is O(log n).

industry_examples.use_case: Sorted product catalog + naive quicksort (last-element pivot) = O(n²) = system freeze. Fix: random pivot (shuffle input first, or pick random pivot index). After fix: guaranteed O(n log n) expected time regardless of input order. Lesson: always consider what inputs look like in production — not just random inputs.

applications.interview_relevance: Complete answer format: 'Time complexity: O(n log n) average case, O(n²) worst case (when pivot is always min/max, e.g. sorted input). Mitigation: random pivot selection makes worst case astronomically unlikely. Space: O(log n) for recursion stack.' This one answer demonstrates you understand cases, real-world concerns, and mitigations.
applications.real_world_workflow: When choosing an algorithm: (1) What does my input actually look like in production? (2) What is the worst case — can it be triggered by real data? (3) For amortized algorithms: does the long-run average matter, or do I need guaranteed per-operation cost? (4) Choose based on realistic inputs, not just theoretical best case.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Saying 'ArrayList.add() is O(1) on average' without using the word 'amortized'. Average case usually refers to random inputs. Amortized refers to cost averaged over a sequence of operations on the SAME data structure.
common_gaps.remediation: Amortized: a single operation might be expensive, but averaged over many operations, cost per operation is low. Example: ArrayList resize is O(n) but happens rarely enough that per-operation average is O(1). Always say 'amortized O(1)' not 'O(1) average' for this case.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Student says 'HashMap.get is O(1) average' — technically correct, but 'amortized' implies a different kind of averaging. HashMap.get O(1) average = random input analysis. ArrayList.add O(1) amortized = sequential operation analysis.

key_takeaways: Big-O = worst case; Ω (Omega) = best case; Θ (Theta) = tight/average bound
Linear search: O(1) best, O(n) worst — target at first vs last position
Quicksort: O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst — sorted input + naive pivot
Fix quicksort worst case: random pivot or shuffle input before sorting
HashMap: O(1) average, O(n) worst — good hash function prevents worst case
Amortized O(1): cost of one operation averaged over a long sequence (ArrayList.add)
ArrayList resizes: 1+2+4+...+n = 2n total cost → O(1) amortized per add
Always state which case: 'O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst' — one sentence says more than just 'O(n log n)'
Production inputs matter: sorted arrays, duplicate-heavy sets, long strings all trigger worst cases

self_check_questions.question: Why is ArrayList.add() described as 'amortized O(1)' rather than just 'O(1)'?
self_check_questions.answer: Because a single call to ArrayList.add() is NOT always O(1). When the internal array is full, add() triggers a resize: copies all existing elements to a new array of double size — that individual call is O(n). 'Amortized O(1)' means: if you call add() n times in total, the sum of all costs is O(n), so the cost per operation averaged over the sequence is O(n)/n = O(1). The expensive resize calls are rare enough (at positions 1, 2, 4, 8, 16...) that they don't change the per-operation average. In contrast, 'O(1) average' would mean 'random inputs make it O(1) on average' — a different kind of averaging.
